does: This paper introduces Transient Turn Injection (TTI), a novel multi-turn attack technique that exploits the stateless nature of LLM moderation systems by distributing adversarial intent across multiple isolated interactions rather than within a single conversation. The authors develop automated attacker agents powered by LLMs to systematically probe and evade safety policies in commercial models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Meta, as well as open-source alternatives. Through extensive blackbox evaluation, they demonstrate significant variations in model robustness to TTI attacks, discovering previously unknown vulnerabilities particularly in medical and sensitive application domains, revealing that only select architectures exhibit substantial inherent resistance to this attack pattern.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on LLM adversarial attacks and jailbreaking, building on prior research that has exposed vulnerabilities in safety fine-tuning and policy enforcement mechanisms. While conventional jailbreak approaches focus on maintaining context within persistent conversations, TTI departs from this paradigm by targeting a distinct gap: the lack of cross-turn memory in moderation systems. The paper connects to research on multi-turn interaction vulnerabilities and builds on work utilizing LLM-powered automated red teaming agents for discovering safety gaps. It also relates to broader research on the brittleness of safety mechanisms in deployed models and the challenges of achieving robust alignment across diverse interaction patterns.
enables: This research highlights a critical architectural vulnerability in current LLM deployment practices, enabling future work on stateful moderation systems that maintain context and policy consistency across turns. The findings motivate the development of improved safety mechanisms that account for distributed adversarial intent across sessions, potentially informing defense strategies that go beyond single-turn filtering. The systematic evaluation framework and identified attack surface patterns provide a foundation for benchmarking model robustness and could drive research into more resilient safety training approaches. This work also opens pathways for investigating whether similar cross-turn vulnerabilities exist in other safety-critical systems and raises questions about the fundamental requirements for robust alignment in stateless architectures.
